findings.description: 5 of 14 link-like elements are placeholder 'Coming Soon' spans with no hrefs — footer nav (GitHub, Documentation, Issues, Changelog) and 'View on GitHub' CTA are all non-functional. Users see navigation items they cannot interact with.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: show navigation-style text with no destination. INSTEAD: either remove the items until they're live, or use disabled links with `aria-disabled='true'` and visually distinct muted styling with a tooltip explaining unavailability.

findings.description: Dark mode toggle does not work on first click — initial state was 'theme-light' and first toggle attempt did not switch. Took a second click to activate dark mode, suggesting a toggle initialization bug.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: ship theme toggles that silently fail on first interaction. INSTEAD: ensure the toggle's initial JS state matches the DOM class so the first click always produces a visible change.

findings.description: Hero stat numbers (29, 22, 19, 16) use different colors (blue, green, orange, red/magenta) which feels random rather than intentional — there's no clear semantic mapping between color and meaning.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: assign arbitrary colors to parallel data points without semantic justification. INSTEAD: use a single accent color for all stat numbers, or map colors to a meaningful scale (e.g., intensity, category).

findings.description: The page follows a very standard SaaS landing page template: hero with stats → pipeline steps → feature cards → agent grid → CTA. No unique visual storytelling, interactive demos, or memorable moments differentiate it from hundreds of similar developer tool pages.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: rely entirely on a conventional section-stacking layout for a developer tool. INSTEAD: add at least one signature interactive element — an animated pipeline visualization, a live terminal demo that types out real output, or a before/after diff view.

findings.description: The terminal/code block (showing `cls feature "feat"`) is very minimal — just a white box with dots. It lacks syntax highlighting, a realistic prompt, and any animation that would make it feel alive.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: show a bare static code block as a 'demo'. INSTEAD: add syntax coloring, a blinking cursor, and ideally a typing animation that reveals the command and its output progressively.

findings.description: The 'Engineering' word in the h1 uses Instrument Serif italic which is elegant but creates a tonal mismatch with the otherwise utilitarian developer-tool positioning. The serif/italic reads as editorial/luxury rather than engineering tooling.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: use high-fashion serif italics for engineering/developer branding unless the entire design language matches. INSTEAD: consider a distinctive monospace or geometric sans accent, or fully commit to the editorial serif aesthetic across all headings.

findings.description: No external links anywhere on the page. Every link is an internal anchor. A user has no way to actually install the plugin, view source code, or access documentation — the 'Install Plugin' CTA just scrolls to a code snippet.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: make the primary CTA a same-page scroll. INSTEAD: link 'Install Plugin' to the actual marketplace listing or GitHub repo with installation instructions. If those aren't ready, use a waitlist/email signup.

findings.description: The agent cards section shows 12 agents in a flat grid with identical card styling. The '+17 more agents' button is also disabled/coming-soon. This creates visual monotony and a dead-end.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: display a flat uniform grid of 12+ items with no visual hierarchy. INSTEAD: group agents by category (security, performance, style) with distinct section headers, or show 4-6 featured agents with expandable 'show all' functionality.

findings.description: The decorative dot/circle pattern behind the hero provides subtle texture but is somewhat generic. The connected-dots motif doesn't clearly relate to the pipeline/multi-agent concept.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: use generic geometric decorations that don't reinforce the product concept. INSTEAD: use a pipeline/flow visualization, agent network graph, or branching tree that echoes the product's multi-agent architecture.

findings.description: Footer shows 'v1.0.0' version but all external links are 'Coming Soon', creating a credibility gap — it claims to be at v1.0 but has no GitHub, docs, or issue tracker.
findings.recommendation: NEVER: display a production version number alongside 'Coming Soon' for essential resources. INSTEAD: use 'Preview' or 'Beta' labeling, or remove the version number until external resources are live.

findings.description: Dark mode is well-implemented with proper dark surfaces and maintained contrast. The blue accent works well in both modes. This is a positive finding.
findings.recommendation: N/A — retain this. The dual-theme support with consistent accent colors is solid.

findings.description: Typography pairing (Instrument Serif for headings, Schibsted Grotesk for body, DM Mono for code) is thoughtful and well-executed. The 72px h1 with -1.8px letter-spacing reads cleanly.
findings.recommendation: N/A — retain this. Good font stack with appropriate weights and spacing.

summary: A competent developer-tool landing page with solid fundamentals: clean layout, good typography pairing, working dark mode, comprehensive hover states with smooth transitions, and proper accessibility attributes (25 aria-labels, skip-link, landmark regions). However, it is undermined by critical UX gaps: no functional external links (GitHub, docs, marketplace), 5 placeholder 'Coming Soon' items in navigation positions, and a primary CTA that only scrolls to a code snippet rather than enabling actual installation. The design follows a standard SaaS template without any distinctive interactive elements or memorable moments, placing it firmly in the 'competent but unremarkable' tier for developer tool sites.
